content.full_text:
TO:                CENTRAL FILE

THRU:              J. PRITCHARD

FROM:              [illegible]

RE:                Code §§ and Regulations Referenced:

                   §17203            §1001(b)           N7200        2502
                   §2030(a)          §1002(d)           N7000        18201


                                      May 13, 1976


                                                                    96-05-123


Mr. Robert E. Gyemant
Attorney at Law
333 Pine Street
Second Floor
San Francisco, California 94104

Re: Olive H. Mayer, City Councilwoman for Woodside, California

Dear Mr. Gyemant:

     Your letter has been forwarded to me for reply.

     Thank you for your letter of April 28, 1976, requesting
an opinion with respect to the interpretation of Government
Code §§87203 and 82030(a). No formal opinion will be issued
because the questions presented do not raise a substantial
question of interpretation under the Political Reform Act.
The following informal comments are offered in an attempt to
help you resolve the matter.

     One of the main purposes of the Political Reform Act is
to attempt to minimize potential conflicts of interest by
public officials. §81001(b) of the Government Code expresses
this sentiment precisely:

     "(b) Public officials, whether elected or
appointed, should perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by
their own financial interests or the finan-
cial interests of persons who have supported
them; ...."

§81002(d) further states:

     "(d) Assets and income of public officials
which may be materially affected by their
official actions should be disclosed and in
appropriate circumstances the officials
should be disqualified from acting in order
that conflicts of interest may be avoided; ...."

Mr. Robert E. Gyemant
May 13, 1976                                                Page Two


     Pursuant to §§87200 and 87203, your client is required
to disclose her investments, her interests in real property,
and income since the previous statement. The Act requires
disclosure of a pro rata share of any income of any trust in
which the individual or spouse owns directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a ten percent interest or greater, worth more
than $1,000.

     A similar disclosure requirement applies to investments
and interests in real property held in trusts. §§82033 and
82034. If the fair market value of filer's pro rata share
of the interest or investment exceeds $1,000.

     The fact situation which you pose requires an examination
of the filer's duty to inquire, in order to properly disclose.
§81004 requires that the filer use "all reasonable diligence."

     "81004. All reports and statements filed under
this title shall be signed under penalty of perjury
and verified by the filer. The verification shall
state that the filer has used all reasonable dili-
gence in its preparation, and that to the best of
his knowledge it is true and complete."


     The requirement of "all reasonable diligence" in your
specific instance could be met by attaching a letter to
Schedule 721-C, in which the facts as they exist at the
time of filing are disclosed; together with a short but
definite statement of the filer's attempts to inquire.

     If you have any further questions, please contact me at
(916) 322-6441.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Alfredo Flores
                                        Staff Attorney
                                        Conflicts of Interest
                                        Division

AF:gb

LAW OFFICES
OF

ROBERT ERNEST GYEMANT
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

333 PINE STREET SECOND FLOOR
TELEPHONE 781-0374 SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94104 CABLE ADDRESS: "GYELAW"
AREA CODE 415 TELEX: 34-248

ROBERT ERNEST GYEMANT
DAVID A. THOMPSON
R. THOMAS FAIR
JUDITH MAYER O'BRIEN

April 28, 1976

Mr. Delbert Spurlock
Chief, Conflict of Interest Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95810

Re: Olive G. Mayer, City Councilperson
for Woodside, California, and
Completion of Statement of Economic
Interest Pursuant to Government
Code Section 87203

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

Our client, Mrs. Olive G. Mayer, is a City Councilperson for the town of Woodside, California. She has requested
our assistance in completing a form of Statement of Economic
Interest. Because of the peculiar fact situation involved,
we are unable to properly advise Mrs. Mayer how to proceed
without the assistence of an advisory opinion pursuant to
Section 83114 of the Government Code.

We are specifically concerned with an interpretation
of Section 87203 of the Government Code which requires that
every city councilperson file a Statement of Economic Interest
disclosing income earned during the period since the date
of filing the previous statement. "Income" is defined under
Section 82030(a) of the Government Code to include a pro rata
share of any income of any trust in which the individual
or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a
10% interest or greater.

We would appreciate your advisory opinion concurring
with our approach to complying with this disclosure requirement
in light of the following facts:

Mr. Delbert Spurlock
April 28, 1976
Page 2

Mrs. Mayer is a beneficiary of a trust established
under the laws of the Bahamian government. We believe that
the trust owns assets in the Untied States, but the trustee
has never disclosed, and now refuses to disclose, what assets
the trust owns, or the amount and nature of its annual income.
For the past year and a half the trustee has chosen to make no
distributions whatsoever to Mrs. Mayer.

In 1973 the Bahamian government revoked the license
of the trust company which acts as trustee of Mrs. Mayer's
trust, and we are informed that the company is now operating
in Great Britain. However, the trustee has proved to be very
elusive and uncooperative and we find it very difficult to
obtain any information from it at all.

We have advised our client to report upon other
income outside of the trust and to disclose the foregoing
situation by attaching a copy of this letter to Schedule 721-C,
the statement of Economic Interest. In our opinion, the particular
requirements of Section 87203 are met by bona fide disclosures
of known income and good faith attempts to quantify the extent
and nature of the income from the trust. We would appreciate
an early response so that we might file an amended Statement
if you are unable to concur in our opinion.

Thank you for your assistance.

Yours very truly,

LAW OFFICES OF
ROBERT ERNEST GYEMANT,
A Professional Corporation


By Robert Ernest Gyemant

cc: Ms. Irma Lewis, Town Clerk
Woodside Town Hall
2925 Woodside Road
Woodside, CA 94062

Mr. Fredrick Caploce, City Attorney
Atkinson, Farasyn & Smith
660 W. Dana
Mountain View, CA

sections.conclusion: No formal opinion will be issued because the questions do not raise a substantial question of interpretation under the Political Reform Act. The requirement of 'all reasonable diligence' under §81004 could be met by attaching a letter to Schedule 721-C disclosing facts as they exist at the time of filing, together with a statement of the filer's attempts to inquire.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a public official meet disclosure requirements under the Political Reform Act when the trustee of a foreign trust refuses to disclose asset and income information, and does good faith disclosure of known income with documentation of attempted inquiry satisfy §87203?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined no formal opinion was necessary, but provided informal guidance that the 'all reasonable diligence' requirement can be satisfied by disclosing known facts and documenting good faith efforts to obtain information from an uncooperative trustee through a letter attached to Schedule 721-C.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This is an FPPC response letter. The document includes both the incoming request letter from the attorney (dated April 28, 1976) and the FPPC's response letter from Alfredo Flores (dated May 13, 1976). The FPPC declined formal opinion but provided substantive informal guidance. Some OCR artifacts present but document content is largely legible.

Mayer, City Councilwoman for Woodside, California Dear Mr. Gyemant: Your letter has been forwarded to me for reply. Thank you for your letter of April 28, 1976, requesting an opinion with respect to the interpretation of Government Code §§87203 and 82030(a). No formal opinion will be issued because the questions presented do not raise a substantial question of interpretation under the Political Reform Act. The following informal comments are offered in an attempt to help you resolve the matter. One of the main purposes of the Political Reform Act is to attempt to minimize potential conflicts of interest by public officials. §81001(b) of the Government Code expresses this sentiment precisely: "(b) Public officials, whether elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the finan- cial interests of persons who have supported them; ...." §81002(d) further states: "(d) Assets and income of public officials which may be materially affected by their official actions should be disclosed and in appropriate circumstances the officials should be disqualified from acting in order that conflicts of interest may be avoided; ...." Mr. Robert E. Gyemant May 13, 1976 Page Two Pursuant to §§87200 and 87203, your client is required to disclose her investments, her interests in real property, and income since the previous statement. The Act requires disclosure of a pro rata share of any income of any trust in which the individual or spouse owns directly, indirectly or beneficially, a ten percent interest or greater, worth more than $1,000. A similar disclosure requirement applies to investments and interests in real property held in trusts. §§82033 and 82034. If the fair market value of filer's pro rata share of the interest or investment exceeds $1,000. The fact situation which you pose requires an examination of the filer's duty to inquire, in order to properly disclose. §81004 requires that the filer use "all reasonable diligence." "81004. All reports and statements filed under this title shall be signed under penalty of perjury and verified by the filer. The verification shall state that the filer has used all reasonable dili- gence in its preparation, and that to the best of his knowledge it is true and complete." The requirement of "all reasonable diligence" in your specific instance could be met by attaching a letter to Schedule 721-C, in which the facts as they exist at the time of filing are disclosed; together with a short but definite statement of the filer's attempts to inquire. If you have any further questions, please contact me at (916) 322-6441. Sincerely, Alfredo Flores Staff Attorney Conflicts of Interest Division AF:gb LAW OFFICES OF ROBERT ERNEST GYEMANT A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION 333 PINE STREET SECOND FLOOR
embedding.summary: An attorney requested FPPC interpretation regarding disclosure obligations for a public official who is a beneficiary of a foreign trust whose trustee refuses to disclose asset and income information. The FPPC declined to issue a formal opinion but provided informal guidance that attaching documentation of good faith inquiry attempts to the Statement of Economic Interest satisfies the reasonable diligence requirement.
